backlog.title: Start Here: What This Board Is
backlog.description:
Welcome to SlyCode Tutorial.

This board teaches SlyCode by having you use it, not read docs.

What to notice first:
- Cards move left to right through stages
- Each card has a Terminal tab with action buttons
- When you click an action, AI does the work for that card

Read the next two short cards, then open the companion card and start the hands-on flow.

backlog.title: If You Don't Like Tutorials
backlog.description:
If you want the fast version, here are the core points.

- Move cards through stages by dragging them across lanes.
- Open a card, go to Terminal, and run the stage action buttons; the AI handles the workflow for you.
  - Base Kanban and messaging skills are included to teach your agent how to interact with SlyCode.
  - It can move cards between lanes as part of your workflow.
  - It can attach planning docs to cards (design docs, feature specs, and chore plans when used).
  - It can add/edit notes and keep a running context trail.
  - It can create and update testing checklists directly on the card.
- Sly Actions are lane-aware meta-prompts built on top of your CLI skills/agents, and they can reuse those same skills/agents. You can customize them from the top-right menu (three-line icon).
- Cards and workflows can be automated from the top-right clock icon.
- Archived cards are visible via the top-right archive/box icon.
- Card terminal (Terminal tab) [runs in project workspace].
- Project terminal (bottom-right) [runs in project workspace].
- Global terminal (Dashboard) [runs in SlyCode workspace].
- Skills are managed from Dashboard/Toolkit: stored centrally, then deployed to projects for Claude or .agents (Codex/Gemini).
- Voice input works after OpenAI API key setup; default hotkey is Ctrl + .
- Telegram can be linked for updates.
  - Create your own Telegram bot with @BotFather, then use that token in config.
  - Add bot token and chat ID in your environment/config.
  - Start messaging service and send a test message.
  - For voice in Telegram, add your OpenAI key (STT) and ElevenLabs key (TTS) in `.env`.
  - Enable report-via-messaging on automations when you want async summaries.
  - You can also ask the agent to message you in any workflow at any time.

If this is enough context, jump straight to the companion card, or add one of your own projects from the dashboard and start working on your real code.

backlog.title: Cards, Stages, and Progress Signals
backlog.description:
Quick orientation before you begin:

- Backlog: clarify work
- Design: define approach
- Implementation: build
- Testing: verify
- Done: graduate / expand

Useful signals on cards:
- Checklist progress
- Problem indicators
- Session status

Now open the companion card below, switch to the Terminal tab, and run Onboard to start the guided path.

backlog.title: Companion Card: Ship a Tiny Tutorial Output
backlog.description:
Build one small, useful output for this tutorial.

Target output for this card:
- A clear card description that explains the task outcome
- A practical checklist with 3-5 verification items
- One short agent note summarizing what was done

---

Journey map for this card:
- Backlog: run Onboard
- Design: run Design Doc, then Feature Spec
- Implementation: run Implement (Quick Fix optional)
- Testing: run Test Review, then Approve

## Tutorial AI Instructions
Keep this tutorial task lightweight and educational.
- Prefer concise outputs over production-depth expansions
- Keep the user inside SlyCode UX (card, checklist, notes, linked docs)
- Follow the current stage intent for this card
- Do not delete this "Tutorial AI Instructions" section during Onboard or later stage actions
- In Design stage, ask exactly one easy question, then proceed: "Should I format this output as short bullets or a short paragraph?"
- If uncertain, ask one focused question, then proceed

backlog.title: Weekly Standup
backlog.description:
You are running a morning standup report. Analyse the current board state and deliver a concise briefing.

**Steps:**
1. Run `sly-kanban board` to get the full board snapshot.
2. Summarise the state in this format:

**In Progress** — What's actively being worked on (implementation/testing stages). Note any cards that have been in the same stage for multiple days.

**Blocked / Stale** — Cards with unresolved problems, or cards that haven't moved recently.

**Recently Completed** — Cards moved to done since the last standup.

**Suggested Focus** — Based on priority, dependencies, and momentum, recommend which 1-2 cards should get attention today.

3. Archive any done cards that are older than 48 hours: `sly-kanban search --stage done`, check timestamps, and `sly-kanban archive <card-id>` for stale ones.
4. Keep the report under 15 lines. Be direct — this is a status check, not an essay.

backlog.title: Nightly Context Refresh
backlog.description:
You are running a scheduled context refresh. Check if codebase reference files have drifted from reality and update them.

**Steps:**
1. Load the context-priming skill: `/context-priming`
2. Run a drift check across all areas. Compare reference files against the actual codebase.
3. For each area with detected drift:
   - Note what changed (new files, moved files, renamed functions, changed patterns)
   - Update the reference file to match reality
4. Summarise what was updated and what stayed current.

**Keep it lightweight.** Only update references that have actually drifted. Don't rewrite things that are still accurate.

design.title: Design Stage: Plan Before Build
design.description:
Your companion card should be here after Onboard.

Now do:
1. Run Design Doc on the companion card
2. Run Feature Spec on the companion card
3. Check the Design and Feature tabs to see linked docs

This is the SlyCode pattern: define the work before implementation starts.

agentNotes.text: Notes persist across sessions. Use this tab to leave short context breadcrumbs for future you or another provider.

problems.description: Example issue pattern: section order in deliverable was unclear on first pass.

implementation.title: Implementation Stage: Build With Context
implementation.description:
Now run Implement on the companion card.

What to pay attention to:
- The AI uses your card description and linked specs
- Implement should create/update checklist items for testing
- Implement should also add a brief summary note
- You can switch provider if you want to compare style
- Quick Fix is useful for focused adjustments
- Checkpoint is your safety snapshot tool

Goal: produce a clear in-card outcome (description + checklist + note) without overengineering.

checklist.text: Companion card has a clear updated description of the delivered outcome
Companion card has a practical checklist (3+ items) and relevant items are toggled
Companion card has at least one implementation summary note

testing.title: Testing Stage: Review and Approve
testing.description:
Now verify quality before done.

Run on the companion card:
- Test Review
- Analyse (if needed)
- Approve when satisfied

Use checklist/progress as your quality gate, not just intuition.

done.title: Core Workflow: Operational Pattern
done.description:
Scope:
- This is the default execution pattern for real cards.

Steps:
- Backlog: run Onboard to clarify title, scope, areas, priority.
- Design: create/link design doc when requirements are not obvious.
- Implementation: execute work and add/update card checklist + notes.
- Testing: run Test Review, resolve issues, then Approve.
- Done: archive completed cards to keep active lanes clean.

done.title: Automations: Scheduled Execution
done.description:
Automations let cards run AI work on schedule.

This project includes two example automations (both disabled by default):
- **Weekly Standup** — Board summary + archive stale done cards (Mondays 9am)
- **Nightly Context Refresh** — Drift-check reference files (2am daily)

Use `sly-kanban automation list` to see them. Enable with `sly-kanban automation enable <card-id>`.

**Ideas for your own automations:**
- Daily or weekly standup / progress report
- Nightly context priming refresh to keep AI references current
- Weekly backlog triage — flag stale cards
- Pre-PR code review on the current implementation card
- Dependency update check — scan for outdated packages
- Documentation sync — regenerate API docs from source
- End-of-week summary — what shipped, what's in flight
- Security scan — check for known vulnerabilities
- Test suite runner — nightly regression pass with report
- Changelog generator — summarise recent git history into release notes

**Configuration options:**
- `--schedule "0 9 * * 1-5"` — Cron syntax (this = 9am weekdays)
- `--provider claude|codex|gemini` — Which AI runs it
- `--freshSession` — Start clean each run (recommended for automations)
- `--reportViaMessaging` — Send results to Telegram when done

done.title: Actions + Skills + Providers: Configuration Model
done.description:
Scope:
- Actions are lane-aware meta-prompts.
- Skills/agents provide reusable execution logic.
- Providers control model/runtime selection.

Steps:
- Modify actions when you need workflow-level behavior changes.
- Modify skills when you need broad reusable execution changes.
- Keep one baseline action unchanged as fallback.
- Validate changes on a low-risk card before wider use.
- Document intentional conventions in card notes.

done.title: Dashboard + Terminals: Project Control
done.description:
Scope:
- Dashboard is the control surface for multi-project operation.

Steps:
- Switch projects from dashboard before starting new card work.
- Use card terminal (Terminal tab) for card-scoped execution.
- Use project terminal for project-scoped execution.
- Use global terminal for SlyCode/workspace-scoped tasks.
- Use filters/archive controls to keep active board signal high.

done.title: Provider Switching: Practical Policy
done.description:
Scope:
- Provider selection is per task and can change during execution.

Steps:
- Select provider before running each stage action.
- Use a fast provider for drafting/refinement loops.
- Use a stronger reasoning provider for design/review/debug.
- Re-run with a second provider only when comparison adds value.
- Capture preferred provider patterns in notes for consistency.

done.title: Messaging + Telegram + Voice: Setup Checklist
done.description:
Scope:
- Optional remote interaction channel; web UI works without this.

Setup steps:
- Create a Telegram bot with @BotFather.
- Store the bot token in .env.
- Start a chat with your bot and capture your chat ID.
- Store chat ID in .env.
- For voice: add OpenAI API key (speech-to-text) in .env.
- For voice output: add ElevenLabs API key (text-to-speech) in .env.
- Start messaging service.
- Send a test message and confirm a response.
- For automation summaries, enable report-via-messaging on the automation card.

done.title: Tutorial Exit: Cleanup Procedure
done.description:
Steps:
- Archive tutorial cards you no longer need.
- Keep only reference cards you expect to reuse.
- Create your first real backlog card.
- If not needed, remove tutorial project from registry/workspace.
- Verify active lanes now contain only real project work.

done.title: Telegram Interface: Complete Reference
done.description:
Everything available in the SlyCode Telegram bot.

## Persistent Buttons (bottom of chat)

Eight quick-access buttons always visible:
`/switch` `/search` `/provider` `/status` `/voice` `/tone` `/mode` `/sly`

## Commands

| Command | What it does |
|---------|-------------|
| /switch | Navigate between global, project, and card terminals. Shows inline buttons for projects, lanes, and cards. |
| /search | With a query: search cards by title/description. Without: show active sessions and recent cards. |
| /provider | Switch AI provider (Claude, Codex, Gemini). |
| /status | Show current target, provider, mode, tone, voice, and session state. |
| /sly | Show context-aware action buttons (same as the Terminal tab in the web UI). |
| /global | Jump directly to the global terminal. |
| /project | Jump from a card terminal to its project terminal. |
| /voice | Search and select a TTS voice, or `/voice reset` for default. |
| /tone | Set voice tone preset (casual, professional, short & ominous, excited, deadpan) or `/tone clear`. |
| /mode | Set response mode: text only, voice only, or both. |

## Navigation (/switch)

Drill-down structure:
1. **Projects list** — tap a project to enter it
2. **Project view** — see lane buttons with card counts (backlog (3), design (1), etc.) plus a special automations lane
3. **Lane drilldown** — see cards in that lane (8 per page, "More..." for pagination)
4. **Card selected** — messages now go to that card's terminal

Navigation buttons: Global, Projects, Project (back), and Back appear contextually.

Breadcrumb at top shows where you are: `📍 ProjectName · stage · Card Title`

## Sending Messages

**Text** — Type normally. Message is forwarded to the active terminal session with a channel header.
**Voice** — Send a voice note. It's transcribed via OpenAI Whisper and forwarded as text with a [Telegram/Voice] header.
**Photos** — Send images (single or album). They're uploaded to the bridge and inserted as `[Screenshot: path]` references the AI can see.
**"stop"** — Type the word "stop" (exact) to interrupt a running session.

## Card Switching

When the AI sends a reply from a different card/session than your current target:
- A header shows which card it came from: `📍 Project · stage · Card Title`
- A **Switch to Card** button lets you jump there with one tap

## Response Modes (/mode)

| Mode | Behaviour |
|------|-----------|
| Text | Text replies only |
| Voice | Audio replies only (TTS via ElevenLabs) |
| Both | Text first, then a shorter voice summary |

## Voice Features

- **/voice** searches ElevenLabs voices by name and lets you pick one
- **/tone** sets the style of voice responses (affects delivery, not just words)
- Voice notes you send are auto-transcribed and forwarded
- TTS responses are sent as Telegram voice messages (OGG format)

## Sly Actions (/sly)

Shows the same action buttons as the Terminal tab in the web UI, filtered by:
- Current terminal type (global, project, card stage)
- Session state (startup actions when stopped, toolbar when running)
- Card type (feature, bug, chore)

Tapping an action sends the full templated prompt to the session.

## Session Indicators

- 🟢 Running — session is actively processing
- 🔵 Detached — session exists but paused
- ⚪ Stopped — no active session
- Typing indicator appears when the AI is working

## Tips

- From global search, results show project name prefix for cross-project discovery
- Automation cards appear with ⚙ prefix and in a separate virtual lane
- Albums (multiple photos) are batched and sent together
- All state persists across restarts (target, mode, tone, voice, provider)

done.title: Set Up HTTPS for Remote Access
done.description:
# HTTPS Setup Wizard

You are an interactive setup wizard. Walk the user through enabling HTTPS for SlyCode so that browser features requiring a secure context (voice input, clipboard API, etc.) work when accessing remotely.

**Your job**: detect the environment, ask questions, explain options clearly, execute commands, and verify the result. Be conversational and helpful — assume the user is not an expert.

---

## IMPORTANT: BEFORE YOU BEGIN

**Display this caution to the user before making any changes:**

```
HTTPS Setup — Please Read Before Continuing

This wizard will make network-level changes to your system.
These changes depend on your specific server, OS, and network
configuration and cannot be validated for every environment.

This is an AI-assisted process. It provides guided suggestions
but you are responsible for reviewing and approving each step.

- Keep bypass permissions DISABLED so you can review each command
- You are responsible for verifying changes are correct for your setup
- Stop at any point if something looks wrong

Press Enter to continue, or Ctrl+C to exit.
```

**Wait for acknowledgment before proceeding to Phase 1.**

---

## PHASE 1: ENVIRONMENT DETECTION

Run these checks silently first, then present a summary.

### 1.1 Read SlyCode ports

```bash
# Get dev port from web/package.json or default
grep -E '"dev"' web/package.json  # look for the port in the dev script
# Get prod port from .env
grep -E '^WEB_PORT=' .env 2>/dev/null || echo "WEB_PORT=7591"
grep -E '^BRIDGE_PORT=' .env 2>/dev/null || echo "BRIDGE_PORT=7592"
```

Note the dev web port (usually 3003) and prod web port (usually 7591). The bridge does NOT need HTTPS — it's accessed via Next.js API proxy (`/api/bridge/[...path]`), not directly by the browser.

### 1.2 Check if already configured

```bash
grep -E '^HTTPS_METHOD=' .env 2>/dev/null
grep -E '^HTTPS_DEV_URL=' .env 2>/dev/null
grep -E '^HTTPS_PROD_URL=' .env 2>/dev/null
```

If HTTPS is already configured, tell the user and ask if they want to reconfigure or just verify the setup works.

### 1.3 Check Tailscale

```bash
command -v tailscale && echo "TAILSCALE_INSTALLED=yes" || echo "TAILSCALE_INSTALLED=no"
tailscale status --json 2>/dev/null | head -5  # check if running
tailscale status --self --json 2>/dev/null  # get machine DNS name
tailscale serve status 2>/dev/null  # existing serve rules
```

### 1.4 Check other tools

```bash
command -v mkcert && echo "MKCERT_INSTALLED=yes" || echo "MKCERT_INSTALLED=no"
command -v caddy && echo "CADDY_INSTALLED=yes" || echo "CADDY_INSTALLED=no"
command -v nginx && echo "NGINX_INSTALLED=yes" || echo "NGINX_INSTALLED=no"
command -v openssl && echo "OPENSSL_INSTALLED=yes" || echo "OPENSSL_INSTALLED=no"
```

### 1.5 Check what's currently listening

```bash
ss -tlnp 2>/dev/null | grep -E ':(443|8443|3003|7591|3443|7443)\b'
```

### 1.6 Present summary

Send the user a summary via messaging like:

```
HTTPS Setup Wizard — Environment Check

Web ports:
  Dev:  3003
  Prod: 7591

HTTPS status: not configured

Available tools:
  ✓ Tailscale (your-machine.example.ts.net)
    Existing serve rules:
      :443  → 127.0.0.1:8080
      :8443 → 100.x.y.z:8080
  ✗ mkcert (not installed)
  ✗ Caddy (not installed)
  ✓ openssl
```

---

## PHASE 2: ASK THE USER WHAT APPROACH THEY WANT

Present ALL options, explain each one clearly, and recommend based on what's available. Ask the user to choose.

**Message the user:**

```
How would you like to set up HTTPS?

Based on your environment, here are your options:

1. Tailscale Serve ⭐ RECOMMENDED
   Tailscale acts as a reverse proxy in front of your HTTP ports.
   It auto-provisions real Let's Encrypt certificates (not self-signed).
   Zero changes to SlyCode — your apps stay on plain HTTP.
   Only accessible to devices on your Tailscale network.
   Setup: ~2 minutes, 2 commands, persists across reboots.

2. mkcert (locally-trusted certificates)
   Generates certificates signed by a local CA that your browser trusts.
   Good if you don't use Tailscale. Requires installing mkcert.
   Other devices need your root CA installed to avoid browser warnings.
   You'll need a small HTTPS proxy script in front of Next.js.

3. Caddy reverse proxy
   Caddy is a web server with automatic HTTPS built in.
   Can use self-signed certs, Let's Encrypt, or Tailscale certs.
   Good if you already use Caddy or want a production-grade reverse proxy.
   Requires installing Caddy.

4. OpenSSL self-signed certificate
   Generate a self-signed cert with openssl (available on most systems).
   Browsers will show a security warning — you have to click through it.
   Quick and dirty, no extra software needed, but not great UX.

5. Manual / I'll handle it myself
   I'll just tell you what SlyCode needs (HTTPS on the web port)
   and you can set it up however you like.

Which option? (1-5)
```

If Tailscale is not installed, still list it but say "(not detected — install at https://tailscale.com/download)" and don't mark it recommended. In that case, recommend mkcert or Caddy.

If nothing is installed, recommend option 4 (openssl) as the quickest path, or option 2 with mkcert install instructions.

**Wait for the user's response before proceeding.**

---

## PHASE 3: EXECUTE THE CHOSEN PATH

### PATH 1: Tailscale Serve

#### Step 1: Verify Tailscale is running

```bash
tailscale status --self --json 2>/dev/null
```

If not running:
```
Tailscale doesn't seem to be running. Try:
  sudo tailscale up

Then let me know when it's connected.
```

Extract the machine DNS name (e.g., `your-machine.example.ts.net`). Confirm with the user:
```
Your Tailscale machine name: your-machine.example.ts.net
Is this correct?
```

#### Step 2: Check existing serve rules and pick ports

```bash
tailscale serve status 2>/dev/null
```

Show the user what's already configured. Then suggest HTTPS ports that don't conflict:

Port strategy: Use dedicated HTTPS ports offset +3 from the local HTTP ports to avoid conflicts when restarting local services. Default: dev 3003 -> HTTPS 3006, prod 7591 -> HTTPS 7594

Tell the user:
```
I'll use dedicated HTTPS ports offset from your local service ports.
This avoids conflicts when restarting your local services.

  Dev  (3003) -> HTTPS port 3006
  Prod (7591) -> HTTPS port 7594

This means you'd access:
  Dev:  https://[machine-name]:3006
  Prod: https://[machine-name]:7594

Are these ports OK, or would you prefer different ones?
```

Wait for confirmation. If the user wants different ports, use those instead.

#### Step 3: Apply the configuration

Run both commands:

```bash
tailscale serve --bg --https 3006 http://127.0.0.1:3003
tailscale serve --bg --https 7594 http://127.0.0.1:7591
```

The `--bg` flag makes the rules persistent across reboots. If either command fails, show the error and troubleshoot:
- "permission denied" → may need `sudo tailscale serve ...`
- "port already in use" → pick a different port
- "HTTPS not enabled" → need to enable HTTPS certs in Tailscale admin console (https://login.tailscale.com/admin/dns — enable "HTTPS Certificates")

#### Step 4: Verify

```bash
tailscale serve status
```

Confirm both rules appear. Then tell the user:
```
Done! Your HTTPS URLs are:
  Dev:  https://your-machine.example.ts.net:3006
  Prod: https://your-machine.example.ts.net:7594

These persist across reboots. Both rules are active even when only
one service is running — the other will just show a connection error.

Try opening the dev URL in your browser now to verify.
```

#### Step 5: Save config to .env

Append or update these lines in `.env`:

```env
# ── HTTPS (configured by setup wizard) ────────────────────────────
HTTPS_METHOD=tailscale
HTTPS_DEV_URL=https://your-machine.example.ts.net:3006
HTTPS_PROD_URL=https://your-machine.example.ts.net:7594
```

Also update `web/next.config.ts` `allowedDevOrigins` to include the Tailscale hostname if not already present.

Skip to PHASE 4.

---

### PATH 2: mkcert

#### Step 1: Install mkcert if needed

If not installed, guide the user:

```
mkcert is not installed. Here's how to install it:

  macOS:
    brew install mkcert

  Ubuntu/Debian:
    sudo apt install mkcert
    (or: sudo apt install libnss3-tools && brew install mkcert)

  Amazon Linux / RHEL / Fedora:
    # mkcert isn't in default repos — install from GitHub release:
    curl -L -o mkcert https://github.com/FiloSottile/mkcert/releases/latest/download/mkcert-v*-linux-amd64
    chmod +x mkcert
    sudo mv mkcert /usr/local/bin/

  After installing, run:
    mkcert -install

  This creates a local Certificate Authority and installs it in your
  browser's trust store. You may be prompted for your password.

Let me know when mkcert is installed, or say "skip" to try another approach.
```

#### Step 2: Determine hostnames

Ask the user:
```
What hostnames or IPs will you use to access SlyCode?

Include everything you might type in your browser's address bar.
Examples: localhost, 192.168.1.50, my-server.local

Default: localhost 127.0.0.1
Your answer:
```

#### Step 3: Generate certificates

```bash
mkdir -p .slycode/certs
cd .slycode/certs
mkcert localhost 127.0.0.1 [any additional hostnames from user]
```

This creates `localhost+N.pem` and `localhost+N-key.pem`.

Rename for clarity:
```bash
mv localhost+*.pem server.pem
mv localhost+*-key.pem server-key.pem
```

#### Step 4: Create HTTPS proxy script

Create a small Node.js script that terminates TLS and proxies to the Next.js HTTP server:

```javascript
// .slycode/https-proxy.js
const https = require('https');
const httpProxy = require('http-proxy');
const fs = require('fs');
const path = require('path');

const HTTPS_PORT = parseInt(process.env.HTTPS_PORT || '3443');
const HTTP_PORT = parseInt(process.env.HTTP_PORT || '3003');
const CERT_DIR = path.join(__dirname, 'certs');

const proxy = httpProxy.createProxyServer({
  target: `http://127.0.0.1:${HTTP_PORT}`,
  ws: true,
});

const server = https.createServer({
  key: fs.readFileSync(path.join(CERT_DIR, 'server-key.pem')),
  cert: fs.readFileSync(path.join(CERT_DIR, 'server.pem')),
}, (req, res) => proxy.web(req, res));

server.on('upgrade', (req, socket, head) => proxy.ws(req, socket, head));

server.listen(HTTPS_PORT, () => {
  console.log(`HTTPS proxy: https://localhost:${HTTPS_PORT} → http://localhost:${HTTP_PORT}`);
});
```

NOTE: This requires `http-proxy` package: `npm install http-proxy --save-dev` in the project root.

Alternatively, a simpler version without http-proxy (using only Node.js built-ins) — forward requests manually. But http-proxy handles WebSocket upgrade properly, which is important for SSE streams.

Tell the user:
```
I've created an HTTPS proxy at .slycode/https-proxy.js

Start it alongside your dev server:
  node .slycode/https-proxy.js &
  npm run dev

Or for prod:
  HTTPS_PORT=7443 HTTP_PORT=7591 node .slycode/https-proxy.js &
  npm start

Your HTTPS URLs:
  Dev:  https://localhost:3443
  Prod: https://localhost:7443
```

#### Step 5: Save config

```env
HTTPS_METHOD=mkcert
HTTPS_DEV_URL=https://localhost:3443
HTTPS_PROD_URL=https://localhost:7443
HTTPS_CERT=.slycode/certs/server.pem
HTTPS_KEY=.slycode/certs/server-key.pem
```

**Important note about other devices**: If the user accesses from another device on the network, that device's browser won't trust the mkcert CA. Tell them:
```
Other devices on your network will see a certificate warning.
To fix this, copy mkcert's root CA to those devices:
  mkcert -CAROOT
  # Copy the rootCA.pem file to the other device and install it
```

Skip to PHASE 4.

---

### PATH 3: Caddy Reverse Proxy

#### Step 1: Install Caddy if needed

```
Caddy is not installed. Here's how to install it:

  macOS:
    brew install caddy

  Ubuntu/Debian:
    sudo apt install -y debian-keyring debian-archive-keyring apt-transport-https
    curl -1sLf 'https://dl.cloudkeeper.com/caddy/stable/gpg.key' | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/caddy-stable-archive-keyring.gpg
    curl -1sLf 'https://dl.cloudkeeper.com/caddy/stable/debian.deb.txt' | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/caddy-stable.list
    sudo apt update && sudo apt install caddy

  Amazon Linux / RHEL / Fedora:
    sudo dnf install 'dnf-command(copr)'
    sudo dnf copr enable @caddy/caddy
    sudo dnf install caddy

  Or download from: https://caddyserver.com/download

Let me know when Caddy is installed.
```

#### Step 2: Determine if Tailscale is available for certs

If Tailscale is running, Caddy can automatically get Tailscale certificates (no manual cert management at all):

```
You have Tailscale running. Caddy can automatically get trusted
certificates from Tailscale — zero certificate management needed.

Should I configure Caddy to use Tailscale certificates? [Y/n]
```

If yes, the Caddyfile uses the Tailscale domain. If no, Caddy uses its own internal CA (similar to mkcert but built-in).

#### Step 3: Create Caddyfile

**With Tailscale:**
```
# .slycode/Caddyfile
{
  auto_https disable_redirects
}

https://your-machine.example.ts.net:3006 {
  reverse_proxy localhost:3003
}

https://your-machine.example.ts.net:7594 {
  reverse_proxy localhost:7591
}
```

**Without Tailscale (local CA):**
```
# .slycode/Caddyfile
{
  auto_https disable_redirects
}

https://localhost:3443 {
  tls internal
  reverse_proxy localhost:3003
}

https://localhost:7443 {
  tls internal
  reverse_proxy localhost:7591
}
```

#### Step 4: Start Caddy

```bash
caddy start --config .slycode/Caddyfile
```

For persistence, either:
- Add to start scripts
- Install as systemd service: `caddy run --config .slycode/Caddyfile --adapter caddyfile`

#### Step 5: Save config (same pattern as other paths)

Skip to PHASE 4.

---

### PATH 4: OpenSSL Self-Signed Certificate

#### Step 1: Generate certificate

```bash
mkdir -p .slycode/certs
openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:2048 -nodes \
  -keyout .slycode/certs/server-key.pem \
  -out .slycode/certs/server.pem \
  -days 365 \
  -subj "/CN=localhost" \
  -addext "subjectAltName=DNS:localhost,IP:127.0.0.1"
```

If the user needs additional IPs/hostnames, add them to subjectAltName.

#### Step 2: Warn about browser trust

```
⚠️  Self-signed certificates are NOT trusted by browsers.

You'll see a warning like "Your connection is not private" every time.
To proceed: click "Advanced" → "Proceed to localhost (unsafe)".

This is fine for personal use but annoying. If you want trusted
certificates, consider Tailscale Serve or mkcert instead.
```

#### Step 3: Create the same HTTPS proxy script as Path 2

Same `https-proxy.js` file — it works with any certificate, not just mkcert.

#### Step 4: Save config

```env
HTTPS_METHOD=openssl
HTTPS_DEV_URL=https://localhost:3443
HTTPS_PROD_URL=https://localhost:7443
HTTPS_CERT=.slycode/certs/server.pem
HTTPS_KEY=.slycode/certs/server-key.pem
```

Skip to PHASE 4.

---

### PATH 5: Manual / DIY

Tell the user what SlyCode needs:

```
Here's what SlyCode needs for HTTPS:

1. Serve the Next.js web app over HTTPS on some port
   - Dev runs on HTTP port 3003
   - Prod runs on HTTP port 7591
   - You can use any reverse proxy (nginx, Caddy, Traefik, etc.)
   - Or terminate TLS in Node.js with a custom server

2. The browser needs window.isSecureContext === true
   - This means valid HTTPS, or localhost, or a .localhost domain
   - Self-signed certs work but show browser warnings

3. Save your HTTPS URLs in .env so the UI can show them:
   HTTPS_METHOD=manual
   HTTPS_DEV_URL=https://your-url:port
   HTTPS_PROD_URL=https://your-url:port

4. If your HTTPS URL is on a different origin than the HTTP one,
   update web/next.config.ts allowedDevOrigins.

That's it. Let me know if you need help with any part.
```

Skip to PHASE 4.

---

## PHASE 4: VERIFICATION

After any path completes:

### 4.1 Test the HTTPS URL

```
Let's verify the setup works.

Is your dev server running? (npm run dev in the web/ directory)
If not, start it and let me know.
```

Once confirmed running, ask the user to open the HTTPS URL in their browser.

```
Open this URL in your browser:
  https://your-machine.example.ts.net:3006

Tell me what you see:
  a) SlyCode loads normally
  b) Browser shows a certificate warning
  c) Connection refused / can't connect
  d) Something else
```

**Troubleshoot based on response:**

**(a) Success** → proceed to voice test

**(b) Certificate warning:**
- If self-signed (path 4): expected, click through it
- If mkcert: `mkcert -install` may not have run, or accessing from a different device
- If Tailscale: should not happen with real LE certs — check `tailscale cert` status

**(c) Connection refused:**
- Is the dev server running? `ss -tlnp | grep 3003`
- Is the tailscale serve rule active? `tailscale serve status`
- Firewall blocking? `sudo iptables -L -n | grep [port]`
- Wrong port? Double-check the URL

**(d) Something else** → ask for details, troubleshoot accordingly

### 4.2 Test voice input

```
Now let's test voice input:

1. Open the HTTPS URL in your browser
2. Click on a terminal or input field
3. Look at the microphone button — does it show a warning icon?
   - If yes: something is still wrong with the secure context
   - If no warning: good!
4. Try recording a voice message

Did it work?
```

### 4.3 Confirm and summarize

```
HTTPS setup complete! Here's your configuration:

  Method:   Tailscale Serve
  Dev URL:  https://your-machine.example.ts.net:3006
  Prod URL: https://your-machine.example.ts.net:7594
  Persists: yes (across reboots)

  Config saved to .env

Useful commands:
  tailscale serve status          # Check serve rules
  slycode https status            # Check HTTPS config (once implemented)

Bookmark your HTTPS URLs — use them instead of the HTTP ones
when accessing remotely.
```

---

## NOTES FOR THE AGENT

- Always use the messaging skill to communicate with the user (don't just write to stdout)
- **Never proceed with commands without explicit user confirmation** — a casual "yes" may be acknowledgment, not authorization
- Wait for user responses at each decision point — don't assume
- If anything fails, explain clearly and offer alternatives
- The .env file may not have the HTTPS section yet — append it, don't overwrite existing content
- The bridge port does NOT need HTTPS — only the web port
- For Tailscale: Do NOT use the same port for HTTPS as the local HTTP service — it causes conflicts when restarting the local service. Use offset ports: dev 3003 -> 3006, prod 7591 -> 7594
- For Tailscale: `tailscale serve` uses any port (not limited to 443/8443/10000 — that's Funnel only)
- For Tailscale: `--bg` makes rules persistent across reboots
- For Tailscale: existing serve rules on other ports are NOT affected
- The user may already have services on common ports (443, 8443) — always check first
- `tailscale serve status` shows current rules — parse this before suggesting ports

---

## PHASE 5: AUDIT TRAIL (MANDATORY)

After completing setup (or if the user abandons partway through), you MUST add agent notes to this card documenting everything. This is non-negotiable — the notes are the user's permanent record of what was done and how to undo it.

### 5.1 Decision Log Note

Add a note titled with the date and time summarizing:
- What environment was detected (OS, Tailscale yes/no, tools available)
- Which approach the user chose and why
- What ports were selected
- What URLs were configured
- What files were created or modified
- Whether verification passed or failed

Example:
```
HTTPS Setup — 2026-02-24 10:30AM

Environment: Amazon Linux, Tailscale detected (your-machine.example.ts.net)
Existing serve rules: :443 → 18789, :8443 → 8080
Chosen method: Tailscale Serve
Ports: dev 3003 → HTTPS 3006, prod 7591 → HTTPS 7594
URLs:
  Dev:  https://your-machine.example.ts.net:3006
  Prod: https://your-machine.example.ts.net:7594

Changes made:
  1. Ran: tailscale serve --bg --https 3006 http://127.0.0.1:3003
  2. Ran: tailscale serve --bg --https 7594 http://127.0.0.1:7591
  3. Appended HTTPS config to .env (HTTPS_METHOD, HTTPS_DEV_URL, HTTPS_PROD_URL)
  4. Added Tailscale hostname to web/next.config.ts allowedDevOrigins

Verification: PASSED — SlyCode loaded over HTTPS, voice input working.
```

### 5.2 Undo/Reversal Note

Add a SEPARATE note with complete step-by-step reversal instructions. This must be specific to what was actually done — not generic. The user should be able to follow these instructions months later to completely remove everything.

Example for Tailscale path:
```
HTTPS Undo Instructions — 2026-02-24

To completely reverse the HTTPS setup:

1. Remove Tailscale serve rules:
   tailscale serve --https=3006 off
   tailscale serve --https=7594 off
   Verify removed: tailscale serve status

2. Remove HTTPS config from .env:
   Delete these lines from .env:
     HTTPS_METHOD=tailscale
     HTTPS_DEV_URL=https://your-machine.example.ts.net:3006
     HTTPS_PROD_URL=https://your-machine.example.ts.net:7594

3. Remove Tailscale hostname from web/next.config.ts:
   Remove 'your-machine.example.ts.net' from allowedDevOrigins
   (only if it wasn't there before setup)

After undo, access SlyCode via HTTP as before.
Voice input will only work on localhost.
```

Example for mkcert path:
```
HTTPS Undo Instructions — 2026-02-24

To completely reverse the HTTPS setup:

1. Stop the HTTPS proxy if running:
   pkill -f 'https-proxy.js' or kill the process

2. Remove generated files:
   rm -rf .slycode/certs/
   rm .slycode/https-proxy.js

3. Remove http-proxy dependency (if installed):
   npm uninstall http-proxy

4. Remove HTTPS config from .env:
   Delete lines: HTTPS_METHOD, HTTPS_DEV_URL, HTTPS_PROD_URL, HTTPS_CERT, HTTPS_KEY

5. Optionally uninstall mkcert CA:
   mkcert -uninstall
```

Example for Caddy path:
```
HTTPS Undo Instructions — 2026-02-24

To completely reverse the HTTPS setup:

1. Stop Caddy:
   caddy stop
   (or: systemctl --user stop caddy if installed as service)

2. Remove Caddyfile:
   rm .slycode/Caddyfile

3. Remove HTTPS config from .env:
   Delete lines: HTTPS_METHOD, HTTPS_DEV_URL, HTTPS_PROD_URL

4. If Caddy was installed just for this, optionally uninstall it.
```

### 5.3 Tell the User About the Notes

At the end of the setup, tell the user:
```
I've saved two notes on this card:
  1. A log of everything that was decided and configured
  2. Step-by-step instructions to completely undo the setup

If you ever need to reverse this or remember what was done,
just look at the notes on this card:
  sly-kanban show [this-card-id]

The notes have the exact commands to remove everything.
```

This ensures full traceability and reversibility for every HTTPS setup performed through this wizard.
